content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
April 23, 199I
James R- Sutton
Neilsen, llerksamer, Hodgson,
Parrinello and MueIler
591 Redwood High$ray
MiIl Valley, cA 9494L
Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-91-094
Dear Mr. Sutton:
You have requested advice under the lobbying provisions, and
with regard to the gift and honoraria limitations of the Political
Reform Act.1/ 
Because your inquiry is of a general nature, we are
treating your request as one for inf orrnal assistance pursuant to
Regulation I8329 (c) (copy enclosed).2/
QUESTIONS
1. May a lobbying f j.rm pay the costs of travel and
attendance at firn functions for an official 
who is the spouse of
firm employee? would the gift lirnitation of Section 85203, and
the disclosure requirement of Section 86II4 apply to the payment?
2. Would paying the expenses of the official 
count toward
the new $250 gift linit 
in Section 89505?
3. Are the answers to the above questions dependent on
whether the lobbying firn is registered to lobby the state board -
or cornmission on which the official 
sits?
L/ covernment Code Sections 81000-9101-5. A11 statutory
references are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. ALl references to regTulations are to
Tit1e 2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2l Informal assi.stance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Strcct, Suite SOL] o P.O. Ror tioT o S:rcranrsrrro CA 95tio4-r)S!'\7 . (()16) 122-5(r6L)


James R. Sutton
Page Tuo
4. If a client of the firm reimburses the firm for the
expenses of an official 
to attend an event, must the firm report
the transaction? In this case, is the gift to the official 
from
the firrn or from the client of the firm?
5. Is the answer to the above question dependent on whether
the clj.ent is registered to lobby the board or commission on which
the official 
sits?
6. In the case of an employee of the firm who is also an
official, 
must the of f icial- discl-ose on his or her statenent of
econonic interests as gifts or income the receipt of travel and
other expenses in connection with attending an event?
7. Must the firm report as rractivity expensesri on its
Iobbying disclosure report salaries and other income paid to
ernptoyees of the firm who are officials?
CONCLUS IONS
1. when a lobbying firm pays the costs of travel and
attendance at a firm event for its employees and the spouses of
the employees, the 9I0 gift limit in Section 86203 and the
requirement to report "activity 
expensesrr do not apply to the
costs attributabLe to the spouse of an employee who is an
official, 
so long as the invitation to the official 
is made as a
part of the firm's regular policy with regard to inviting all
spouses of employees who attend the event.
2. If the payroent of the official's 
expenses in connection
with attending an event are rrgiftsrt to the official, 
the payrnent
will count toward the $250 limitation in Section 89505 on gifts
received by state officials. 
In the analysis we discuss under
what circumstances the palnnent of expenses would be a gift to an -
official 
who is the spouse of a firm ernployee.
3. The $10 gift limit in sections 85203 and 86204, and
requirenent to disclose t'activity expensesrr apply to aIl
Iegislative officials, 
and to officials of state agencies which
are requj.red to be listed on the registration statenent of the
Iobbying f irrn, i.e., those agencies the Iobbying firn or one of
the firm's lobbyists has attempted or is attempting to influence.
l,tembers of a state board or conmission and designated
enpJ-oyees of a state agency are prohibited from accepting gifts
with a total value of more than 5250 in a cal-endar year from any
source if the mernber or employee would be required to report the
receipt of income or gifts frorn that source on his or her
statement of econornic interests.


James R. Sutton
Page Three
4. If a client of the lobbying firn pays for a gift for an
official, 
and the lobbying firm '.arrangesrr the qift (e.g., pays
for the gift and is later reirnbursed by the client), the client is
the actual donor of the gift. 
However, a lobbying f irrn may not
make or arranqe a gift of more than gIO in a calendar rnonth to a
legislative official 
or to certain agency officials. 
A lobbying
firrn r+hich arranges a gift to an official 
must disclose ttre gift
as an activity 
expense -
If a client of the firm reimburses the firrn for an activity
expense, other than a gift, the firn must disclose the activity
expense, and must disclose the fact that the client actually paid
the expense.
5. The $IO gift limit in Sections g6203 and a62O4, and the
requirement to disclose rractivity expensesrr apply to aI).
Iegislative officials, 
and to officiats 
of a state agency which is
required to be listed on the registration statement of the
lobbying firm, i.e., 
those agencies the lobbying firn has been
authorized by any client to attenpt to influence.
6. An enployee of the firm who is an official 
rnust disclose.
as 'rgiftsrr on his or her statement of economic interests the
payments for travel and other expenses received frorn the firrn in
connection with attending a social event. In the event the
official 
has lirnited discl,osure under an agency conflict of
interest code, the requirement to disclose the gift(s) 
depends on
whether the firn is a reportable source of income as desciibed in
the official's 
disclosure category.
7. The firrn must disclose as tractivity expensesl on j.ts
lobbying disclosure report salaries, gifts, 
and other payments
made to any employee of the firn who is a legislative official, 
or
who is an official 
of a state agency which is or should be listed -
on the f irrn,s registration 
statement.
ANALY S I S
Lobbying firms must disclose, among other payments made in
connection rrith attempting to influence legislative or
administrative action, ttactivity expenses.r' (Section
86114 (a) (5) . ) An rractivity expenserr is:
(a) ... Any expense incurred or payment made by a lobbyist,
a lobbying firm, lobbyist ernployer or a person described
in subdivision (b) of Section 85115, or arranged by a
Iobbyist or lobbying firn, which benefits in whole or
in part any elective state officiaJ,, legislative official,
agency official, 
state candidate or a member of the
immediate fanily of one of these individuals. 
Activity


James R- Sutton
Page Four
expenses inctude gifts, honoraria, consulting fees,
sa)-aries, and any other form of cotnpensation but do not
incl-ude campaign contributions.
(b) 
rrAgency officialtr as used in this chapter means any
officiaL of a state agency whose adrninistrative actions
the lobbyist, J-obbying firm, or lobbyist employer, or
person described in subdivision (b) of Section 86115 has
attempted or is atternpting to influence.
Section 8 61II.
As a general rule, when a gift is received by the spouse or
innediate family member of an official-, 
and the official 
enjoys
direct benefit from the gift, the gift will be deemed to have been
received by, and must be disclosed by, the official. 
(Regulation
18726.2.)
Regulation LA726- 2 (a) provides:
Gifts given directly to nembers of an official,s
inmediate family are not gifts to the official 
unless
used or di.sposed of by the official 
or given by the
recipient rnember of the official,s 
imrnediate family
to the official- for disposition or use at the official,s
discretion.
However, if the circumstances surrounding the gift clearly
indicate that the gift was intended for the spouse of the
official, 
and the official- incidentally enjoys benefit from the
gift, 
and there are no circumstances present to indicate that the
donor intended to give a gift to the official, 
then the gift is
not reportable by the official. 
(Conbs Advice Letter, No. A-82-
l-41, copy enclosed.) Rather, the portion of the gift enjoyed by
the official 
is deerned to be from the official,s 
spouse and,
therefore, is not reportable. 
(Section 82028(b) (3).) 
Sinilar1y,
the gift limitations in Sections 86203 and 89505 do not apply to
gifts received by an offici.al from his or her spouse.
A Iobbying firn may not nake or arrange gifts aggregating
more than $I0 in a calendar month to certain persons. Section
86203 provides:
It shalL be unlawful for a lobbyist, or lobbying firm,
to make gifts to one person aggregating more than ten
dollars ($IO) in a calendar rnonth, or to act as an agent
or intermediary in the rnaking of any gift, 
or to arrange
for the naking of any gift by any other person.


Jatnes R. Sutton
Page Five
For purposes of Section 862o3, 'rgiftrr means:
A gift made directly or i.ndirectly to any state candidate,
elected state officer, or legislative official, 
or to an
agency official 
of any agency required to be listed on
the registration statement of the lobbying form or the
lobbyist employer of the l-obbyist.
section 86201.
with regard to disclosure of gifts received fron the firrn by
an employee who is an official, 
if the firm is of the type that
the ernployee is required to disclose as a source of income, the
employee must disclose the gift on his or her statement of
econonic interests. 
(Section a2o28; Section 872o7. ) In addition,
the enployee may not accept gifts with an aggregate value of more
than $250 in a calendar year from the firm if the firn would be
required to be discLosed as a source of income on the employee's
statement of economic interests. 
(Section 89505.)
with regard to disclosure of activity expenses, any payments
rnade by a lobbying firn, or arranged by a lobbying firm, which
benefit a legislative official-, or an agency offici.al as described
above, must be disclosed by the lobbying firm as an "activity
expenserr on its quarterly disclosure report. 
This requirement
includes disclosure of activity 
expenses which benefit an ernployee
of the firm who is an official, 
his or her immediate fanily
member, and pa)rments vhich benefit an inmediate fanily member of
an ernployee if the fanily member is a reportable official.
I hope this letter adequately responds to your questions. If
you have additional questions, please caII me at (916) 322-5662.
Sincerely,
Scott H. Hallabrin
Acting General counsgl 
.

By: '. Jeanne Pritchard
Chief, Technical Assistance
and Analysis Division
